modules.title: Introduction to Prompt Engineering
modules.key_question: What are LLMs and why does prompt engineering matter?
modules.summary: Module 1 introduces the course and the fundamental concept of prompt engineering. It demonstrates the power of ChatGPT through a creative prototyping example (meal planning → Pokemon stories → math game → working code), showing that LLMs are tools for giving ideas form through rapid iteration. The module explains how LLMs work (next-word prediction), why outputs vary between runs (inherent randomness), and what implications this has for prompt design.
main_lecture_titles: What Can You Do with ChatGPT? (Meal Plan Demo)
Why Prompt Engineering Matters (Persona Pattern Preview)
How Large Language Models Work (Next-Word Prediction)
Randomness and Variation in LLM Outputs
patterns_introduced: Persona Pattern (preview only — deep dive in M2)
exam_relevant_facts: LLMs do next-word prediction, not retrieval
LLMs are trained on internet text with a knowledge cutoff
LLM outputs vary between runs — this is by design, not a bug
The 'act as' prefix activates persona behavior
Rapid iteration is the key value of LLM interaction

modules.title: Prompts and Prompt Patterns
modules.key_question: What is a prompt and how do patterns work?
modules.summary: Module 2 defines prompts formally (as calls to action, cues/reminders, and parts of ongoing conversation) and explains the intuition behind why patterns work (LLMs continue patterns they saw in training). The module covers prompt programming — writing rules in natural language that the LLM follows. It introduces the formal concept of a prompt pattern and provides a deep dive into the Persona Pattern with multiple examples (skeptic, Socratic teacher, JavaScript console, Linux terminal).
key_concepts_taught: Prompts as calls to action, cues, and conversation
Pattern activation via training data matches
Persona as system emulation (not just people)
exam_relevant_facts: Prompts can be verbs, adjectives, or nouns
Prompts can affect future outputs through conversational memory
'From now on' establishes persistent rules
Persona Pattern can emulate systems, not just people
Patterns are documented, reusable solutions to specific problems

modules.title: Prompt Patterns I — Question and Interaction Patterns
modules.key_question: How do you get better questions and tailored output?
modules.summary: Module 3 introduces four patterns focused on improving question quality and audience targeting. The Question Refinement Pattern asks the LLM to improve your questions. The Cognitive Verifier Pattern breaks complex questions into sub-questions. The Audience Persona Pattern tailors output to specific readers. The Flipped Interaction Pattern reverses the Q&A flow so the LLM asks you questions. Together, these patterns give you control over how information flows in the conversation.
exam_relevant_facts: Question Refinement: 'suggest a better question and ask me if I would like to use it instead'
Cognitive Verifier: generate sub-questions, answer each, then combine
Audience Persona: 'Assume that I am X' (output is for X)
Persona: 'Act as X' (LLM is X)
Flipped Interaction: end with 'Ask me the first question' to get one-at-a-time behavior

modules.title: Few-Shot Examples and Chain-of-Thought
modules.key_question: How do you teach the LLM new tasks and improve reasoning?
modules.summary: Module 4 covers few-shot prompting (teaching via examples) and reasoning techniques. Few-shot examples can be simple (Input/Output) or include intermediate steps (Think/Action). The lecture covers common mistakes in few-shot (generic prefixes, ambiguous examples). Chain of Thought prompting ('Let's think step by step') dramatically improves accuracy on reasoning tasks. ReAct extends CoT to include external tool use (calculators, search). The module ends with using LLMs to grade each other for automated evaluation.
main_lecture_titles: Using LLMs to Grade Each Other
key_concepts_taught: Pattern continuation as the mechanism of few-shot
Zero-shot CoT ('Let's think step by step')
exam_relevant_facts: Few-shot works because LLMs continue patterns via next-word prediction
Use descriptive prefixes (Surface feeling:) not generic ones (Output:)
CoT improves accuracy by making correct reasoning precede the answer
ReAct loop: Thought → Action → Observation → repeat
CoT most useful for complex reasoning, not simple facts
Few-shot examples teach both decision logic AND output format

modules.title: Prompt Patterns II — Output Control and Brainstorming
modules.key_question: How do you control output format and generate ideas?
modules.summary: Module 5 introduces patterns for output control and creative generation. The Game Play Pattern turns learning into interactive games. The Template Pattern gives precise control over output format via placeholders. The Meta Language Creation Pattern lets you teach the LLM a custom shorthand for efficient communication. The Recipe Pattern fills in missing steps in a partial process. The Alternative Approaches Pattern generates multiple solutions for brainstorming.
exam_relevant_facts: Template pattern uses CAPITALIZED or {bracketed} placeholders
Always add 'preserve the overall formatting' to template prompts
Recipe pattern: you provide start + end, LLM fills in middle
Alternative approaches: ask for pros/cons of each
Meta language: explicitly teach the LLM the shorthand before using it

modules.title: Prompt Patterns III — Advanced Workflows
modules.key_question: How do you build sophisticated, maintainable prompt workflows?
modules.summary: Module 6 covers advanced workflow patterns and pattern composition. The Ask for Input Pattern prevents the LLM from generating unwanted content immediately after setup. Combining multiple patterns is how you build sophisticated prompts. The Outline Expansion Pattern works around LLM size limits via hierarchical outlines. The Menu Actions Pattern defines reusable commands. The Fact Check List Pattern creates a verification workflow for LLM output. The Tail Generation Pattern prevents instruction-forgetting in long conversations. The Semantic Filter Pattern processes text based on meaning.
key_concepts_taught: Control handoff to prevent unwanted generation
exam_relevant_facts: Ask for Input MUST be at the end of the prompt to work
Combining patterns: take statements from each, include them all
Outline Expansion uses numbered sections (3.1, 3.2.1) for self-describing assembly
Tail Generation: 'repeat the rules at the end of every output'
Fact Check List: extract fundamental facts, compare to text for completeness
Menu Actions: 'Whenever I type X, you will do Y'
Semantic Filter: 'rewrite as little as possible' prevents over-rewriting
